1: 'Therefore, since Christ suffered in his body, arm yourselves also with the same attitude, because whoever suffers in the body is done with sin' — the military metaphor of 'arming' (hoplisasthe) applied to the mind/attitude mirrors Paul's 'put on the full armor of God' (Ephesians 6:11-17). The phrase 'done with sin' (pepautai hamartias) has been variously interpreted: most likely it means that suffering functions as a moral purification — the person who commits to the Christ-suffering pattern has made a decisive break with the sin-serving lifestyle of their pre-conversion past (cf. 1 Peter 4:3's description of that past).
2: 'As a result, they do not live the rest of their earthly lives for evil human desires, but rather for the will of God' — the contrast between 'evil human desires' (epithumiais anthrōpōn) and 'the will of God' (thelēmati theou) is the fundamental orientation-choice that conversion requires. The 'rest of their earthly time' (ton epiloipon en sarki chronon) frames the remainder of mortal life as a finite but critical period — the eschatological urgency of 1 Peter 4:7 ('the end of all things is near') makes the orientation-choice maximally significant.
3: 'For you have spent enough time in the past doing what pagans choose to do — living in debauchery, lust, drunkenness, orgies, carousing, and detestable idolatry' — the catalogue of Gentile vices was a standard Jewish polemic against pagan lifestyle (cf. Wis 1 Peter 14:22-27; Galatians 5:19-21; 1 Corinthians 6:9-10). Peter's specific list emphasizes the social-festive dimension of pagan religion: orgies (komois), carousings (potois), and idolatry (athemitois eidōlolatriais). The community's departure from these practices constitutes a social rupture — they no longer participate in the civic and family festivals that centered on sacrifice to the gods and the social drinking and feasting that surrounded them.
4: 'They are surprised that you do not join them in their reckless, wild living, and they heap abuse on you' — the 'abuse' (blasphēmountes, blaspheming, slandering) that former associates heap on the converts expresses the social logic of the honor-shame world: departure from shared religious and social practice is experienced as a judgment against the group that continues it. The converts' refusal to participate is read not as neutral withdrawal but as implicit criticism. Roman-era sources confirm the hostility that greeted Jewish and Christian social non-conformity (Tacitus: 'hatred of the human race').
5: 'But they will have to give account to him who is ready to judge the living and the dead' — the eschatological tribunal is the ultimate context for the community's suffering. The 'ready to judge' (hetoimōs echonti krinai) is the same eschatological urgency as 1 Peter 4:7 ('the end of all things is near'). The formula 'the living and the dead' becomes standard in early Christian creedal formulations (Acts 10:42; 2 Timothy 4:1; the Apostles' Creed: 'he will come to judge the living and the dead'). The dual scope of the judgment means that those who have already died are not exempt from the accounting Peter describes.
6: 'For this is the reason the gospel was preached even to those who are now dead, so that they might be judged according to human standards in regard to the body, but live according to God in regard to the spirit' — the 'dead' who were preached to are most naturally those who have died since their conversion — Christians who received the gospel, lived under social judgment ('judged according to human standards in the body'), and died. The gospel was preached to them so that though they experienced the mortal judgment of bodily death, they might live in the spirit according to God's standard. Peter assures a community that has lost members to death that those deaths are not the final word.
7: 'The end of all things is near. Therefore be alert and of sober mind so that you may pray' — the eschatological urgency (pantōn to telos ēngiken) is the same verb Peter used in 1 Peter 4:5 and the same language as James 5:8 (the coming of the Lord has drawn near) and Revelation 1:3 (the time is near). The sober-mindedness (sōphronesate kai nēpsate, be sensible and be sober) for prayer is not merely practical advice but eschatological preparation: the community alert to the nearness of the end will maintain the prayer-connection to the eschatological God.
8: 'Above all, love each other deeply, because love covers over a multitude of sins' — the citation of Proverbs 10:12 ('love covers all offenses') is introduced with 'above all' (pro pantōn) — the highest priority among the community ethics. The 'covers' is not a euphemism for ignoring sins but the active covering that forgiveness provides: love creates the conditions for mutual forgiveness rather than mutual accusation. In a community under external social pressure, the internal environment of forgiving love is both a practical necessity and a theological embodiment of the gospel they proclaim.
9: 'Offer hospitality to one another without grumbling' — the philoxenia (love of strangers/hospitality) commanded here was essential infrastructure for the early Christian movement: traveling missionaries, persecuted believers, and messengers relied on hospitality networks in the absence of safe commercial inns. The 'without grumbling' (aneu gongysmou) addresses the real burden of hosting: the gongymos was the complaint of the Israelites against Moses in the wilderness (Exodus 16:7-12; Numbers 11:1; 14:27 LXX use gongymos for this grumbling). The hospitality that Peter mandates is cheerfully offered rather than grudgingly provided.
10: 'Each of you should use whatever gift you have received to serve others, as faithful stewards of God's grace in its various forms' — the charisma (grace-gift) and oikonomia (stewardship) framework of Paul (1 Corinthians 12:4-11; Ephesians 4:11-13) appears here as the basis for community service. The 'various forms' (poikilēs charitos theou) of God's grace are the diversity of gifts that constitute the community's mutual service. The stewardship frame: the gifts are not the possessor's own but God's grace entrusted to them for others' benefit — a frame that prevents the spiritual arrogance that Paul also combats in 1 Corinthians.
11: 'If anyone speaks, they should do so as one who speaks the very words of God. If anyone serves, they should do so with the strength God provides, so that in all things God may be praised through Jesus Christ' — the two categories (speaking and serving) encompass the full range of ministry. The speaker who 'speaks the very words of God' (logia theou) bears responsibility analogous to the OT prophet or the apostle — words that claim divine authority require absolute fidelity. The server who acts 'with the strength God provides' (ex ischyos hēs chorēgei ho theos) is the one who channels divine power rather than relying on human capacity alone. The goal: God's praise through Christ.
12: 'Dear friends, do not be surprised at the fiery ordeal that has come on you to test you, as though something strange were happening to you' — the 'fiery ordeal' (pyrōsei) is the same root as the refiner's fire of Malachi 3:2-3 and the furnace of Isaiah 48:10. Peter's primary purpose is pastoral re-framing: the community is tempted to interpret their suffering as divine abandonment or cosmic accident. The instruction 'do not be surprised' (mē xenizesthe) is the counterpart to 1 Peter 4:4's 'they are surprised' (xenizontai) that the community doesn't join in pagan living; the community's lack of surprise mirrors their former associates' surprise — but in the opposite direction.
13: 'But rejoice inasmuch as you participate in the sufferings of Christ, so that you may be overjoyed when his glory is revealed' — the suffering-glory pattern (1 Peter 2:21-25; 1 Peter 3:18-22) returns as the eschatological frame for present experience. 'Participation in the sufferings of Christ' (koinōneite tois tou Christou pathēmasin) is the same koinonia-language that Paul uses in Philippians 3:10 (to know Christ and the fellowship of his sufferings). The suffering is not incidental but structural: it participates in the Christ-pattern that defines the community's existence between the first and second coming.
14: 'If you are insulted because of the name of Christ, you are blessed, for the Spirit of glory and of God rests on you' — the beatitude for suffering in the name of Christ echoes Matthew 5:11 (blessed are you when people insult you and persecute you because of me). The 'name of Christ' (en onomati Christou) is the publicly confessed identification that makes the community a target: 'Christian' (Christianos) was a designation of public identification (Acts 11:26; 1 Peter 26:28), not just an internal self-description, and bearing it was what triggered the abuse of 1 Peter 4:4. The Spirit of glory that rests on them is the eschatological Spirit — the Spirit of the age to come already present in the suffering community.
15: 'If you suffer, it should not be as a murderer or thief or any other kind of criminal, or even as a meddler' — the criminal categories enumerate the standard offenses prosecuted in Roman courts; 'meddler' (allotrioepiskopos, one who oversees others' affairs) is probably the charge of social interference — not keeping to one's own business, meddling in social arrangements. The point: Christian suffering must be specifically for being a Christian, not for genuine criminal conduct or for provocative social intervention. The legal context is the Roman judicial system, in which the community's good conduct should create a clear distinction between Christians and criminals.
16: 'However, if you suffer as a Christian, do not be ashamed, but praise God that you bear that name' — the term 'Christian' (Christianos) appears only three times in the NT (Acts 11:26; 1 Peter 26:28; here). It was likely first used by outsiders as a political-social label (followers of Christos, a potentially seditious political figure in the Roman calculus). Peter takes the outsider label and invests it with honor: bearing the name is an occasion for praise, not shame. The reversal of shame into honor through the theological reframing of suffering is the pastoral heart of 1 Peter.
17: 'For it is time for judgment to begin with God's household; and if it begins with us, what will the outcome be for those who do not obey the gospel of God?' — the sequence of judgment is drawn from Ezekiel 9:6 (begin at my sanctuary) — the divine judgment of Ezekiel's vision begins with the elders at the sanctuary before moving outward. The argument moves from 'we experience the purifying judgment of God's household' to 'if this is what the purifying judgment of the righteous looks like, what will the consuming judgment of the unrighteous look like?' The community's suffering is reinterpreted as the privileged beginning-point of eschatological judgment, not as being forsaken by God.
18: 'If it is hard for the righteous to be saved, what will become of the ungodly and the sinner?' — the quotation is Proverbs 11:31 LXX. The 'hardness' of salvation for the righteous is not soteriological doubt but the observation that the path of faithful discipleship involves real cost, suffering, and refinement — the very suffering that Peter's communities are experiencing. If the path to salvation for those who are righteous is difficult (costly, requiring endurance), then the path for those who are ungodly is not merely difficult but catastrophic. The comparative makes the community's suffering a mark of privilege rather than a mark of divine disfavor.
19: 'So then, those who suffer according to God's will should commit themselves to their faithful Creator and continue to do good' — the closing exhortation for the chapter synthesizes the whole argument: suffering-according-to-God's-will + self-commitment-to-the-faithful-Creator + continuing-good-works. The 'faithful Creator' (pistō ktistē) is a distinctive title: God as Creator who keeps faith with what he has made. The Creator's faithfulness grounds the entrusting of suffering — the God who made the world and sustains it can be trusted with the community's lives. The exhortation 'continue to do good' (en agathopoiia) returns to the public-witness strategy of 2:12 and 1 Peter 3:17 — visible goodness as both witness and protection.
